stories.headline: Avalanche trade Samuel Girard, second-round pick to Penguins for Brett Kulak
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche made a significant blueline move Tuesday, trading defenseman Samuel Girard and a 2028 second-round pick to the Pittsburgh Penguins in exchange for veteran defenseman Brett Kulak, ten days before the NHL trade deadline.

Girard, 27, was the longest-tenured member of Colorado's defense corps, having arrived via trade from Nashville in November 2017. He played 583 regular-season games and 67 playoff games for the Avalanche, including the team's 2022 Stanley Cup championship run. His contract carries a $5 million cap hit and runs through next season.

Kulak, 32, brings more than 600 games of regular-season NHL experience and three Stanley Cup Final appearances, including back-to-back trips with Edmonton in the past two seasons. The left-handed defenseman carries a $2.75 million cap hit on an expiring contract, providing Colorado a $2.25 million cap savings over Girard.

'In Brett, we are acquiring a two-way defenseman who can play up and down the lineup,' Avalanche general manager Chris MacFarland said. 'He logs big minutes in all situations and brings a ton of playoff experience that will help our blue line depth.' Coach Jared Bednar called Girard 'an unbelievable teammate' while expressing confidence the deal benefits both parties.

stories.headline: Denver City Council gives initial approval to ban on masked law enforcement officers during arrests
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council voted unanimously Monday on first reading to ban all law enforcement officers, including federal immigration agents, from wearing facial coverings while detaining or arresting individuals. The measure also requires officers to wear visible identification at all times during enforcement actions.

All 13 council members supported the ordinance, which was sponsored by Councilwomen Flor Alvidrez and Shontel Lewis. The legislation was prompted by reports of masked agents conducting immigration enforcement operations across the country. Mayor Mike Johnston, who met with council members last week, indicated he supports the measure and intends to sign it.

The ordinance still requires a final vote next week before taking effect. Officers who fail to comply could face a citation with a penalty of up to a $99 fine or 300 days in jail. The Denver Police Department would be responsible for enforcement and said it is developing an implementation plan that prioritizes de-escalation.

Denver would join cities including Los Angeles, Seattle, New York, Chicago and St. Paul in passing laws aimed at restricting ICE operations during President Trump's second term. A similar masking law in California was struck down by a federal judge earlier this month on constitutional grounds, though supporters of the Denver ordinance believe its universal application to all levels of law enforcement will help it withstand legal challenges.

stories.headline: Denver police arrest suspect in two separate attacks on women in January
stories.summary:
Denver police announced Monday the arrest of Chanse Leon Lipscomb, 24, in connection with two separate assaults on women that occurred in January in the south Denver area. Lipscomb was taken into custody Saturday and is being held for investigation of second-degree assault, third-degree assault and second-degree attempted kidnapping.

The first incident occurred January 7 around 5:30 p.m. in the 1300 block of South Grant Street, where Lipscomb allegedly attacked a woman before a bystander intervened and helped the victim escape. The second attack took place January 27 near South Grant Street and East Ellsworth Avenue, where a woman who was running fought back and screamed until the suspect fled.

Investigators released information about the two incidents on January 30, warning residents to remain vigilant. Police said physical and forensic evidence gathered from both crime scenes led them to identify Lipscomb as the suspect. No motive has been released.

The arrest concludes a weeks-long investigation into the incidents, which drew community concern due to their proximity and the vulnerability of the victims targeted in public spaces.

stories.headline: Wildfire near Franktown prompts evacuations in Douglas County before being fully contained
stories.summary:
A grass fire ignited Tuesday near Dahlberg Road and Lake Gulch Road in Douglas County, approximately eight miles east of where Interstate 25 passes through the Larkspur area. The blaze grew from an initial estimate of five acres to 50 acres within roughly an hour, prompting an evacuation order for residents between Lake Gulch and Greenland and between Dahlberg Road and Highway 83.

The Douglas County Office of Emergency Management requested aerial firefighting resources, and private citizens were asked to avoid the area. The fire was burning under dangerous conditions, with strong winds and dry air contributing to an active red flag warning across much of eastern Colorado.

Franktown Fire reported the blaze was 100 percent contained by mid-afternoon Tuesday, and the Douglas County Sheriff's Office lifted the evacuation order. Mop-up operations were continuing as of the latest update.

The fire comes amid a stretch of elevated fire weather across Colorado's Front Range and Eastern Plains, with meteorologists warning of critical fire conditions through Friday due to gusty winds and low relative humidity.

stories.headline: Laura Clellan confirmed as first female director of Colorado Parks and Wildlife since agency merger in 2011
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife commissioners voted unanimously Monday to permanently appoint Laura Clellan as agency director, making her the first woman to hold the position since CPW was formed through the merger of Colorado Parks and Colorado Division of Wildlife in 2011. Clellan had been serving in the role on an acting basis since December 1.

Clellan was selected from roughly 150 applicants, with five finalists advancing to stakeholder interviews. Commissioners cited her strong leadership skills, her prior work with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service in Lakewood, and her steady management of the agency during a period of significant transition. She brings more than three decades of military service, including the rank of major general in the Colorado Army National Guard and previous service as adjutant general of the Colorado Department of Military and Veterans Affairs.

Clellan assumes the directorship at a difficult moment for the agency. The wolf reintroduction program has been halted by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service indefinitely, and stakeholder groups are engaged in disputes over furbearer management regulations. Some hunting advocacy organizations have expressed concern that the Polis administration has been sidelining their interests through appointments and policy.

Dan Gates, executive director of Coloradans for Responsible Wildlife Management, said his group is confident Clellan will provide equitable leadership. Conservation groups including the Rocky Mountain Wolf Project and the Center for Biological Diversity also expressed optimism, while acknowledging the agency faces significant challenges ahead.

stories.headline: Nuggets' clutch-game struggles draw concern from Jokic as defensive numbers rank among NBA's worst
stories.summary:
Denver Nuggets center Nikola Jokic expressed open concern about the team's late-game struggles following another close loss to the Golden State Warriors on Sunday in San Francisco, adding to a pattern that has defined Colorado's NBA season. Denver has won just 14 of 29 clutch games — defined as games within five points in the final five minutes — a win rate that ranks in the middle of the league.

The defensive numbers in clutch situations are particularly alarming. Denver's defensive rating in those situations ranks 29th in the NBA, ahead of only the Brooklyn Nets. That drag pulls the team's overall clutch net rating to 27th, ahead of only the Pelicans, Grizzlies and Nets. The team also ranks 26th in assist-to-turnover ratio during clutch minutes, with late turnovers contributing directly to Sunday's loss.

One statistical anomaly complicates the picture: Denver is 8-2 in clutch games without Jokic and 6-13 with him, a counterintuitive split that has drawn notice around the league. 'I don't know what it is,' Jokic said. 'But we need to figure it out.'

Guard Christian Braun attributed the struggles to execution rather than process, saying the team needs to hold its spots and be tougher down the stretch. The Nuggets' league-best offensive rating drops from first to seventh in clutch situations, compounding the defensive lapses that have cost Denver multiple winnable games this season.

stories.headline: Defense Secretary Hegseth visits Denver-area space companies, pledges to cut Pentagon bureaucracy for smaller firms
stories.summary:
Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth visited Colorado Monday as part of his Arsenal of Freedom Tour, stopping at True Anomaly, a Denver-area space company, and Sierra Space to highlight his administration's push to expand defense contracting opportunities for smaller businesses. Hegseth said the nation's defense posture cannot rely on five major contractors and criticized the practice of decade-long sole-source contracts that cost billions before delivering a product.

'If we are going to have space dominance and deterrence, we need to unleash every company possible,' Hegseth said. 'One of the biggest wars that we are fighting is against our own Pentagon bureaucracy every single day.' He promised reforms to make it easier for smaller defense firms to compete for contracts.

Separately, Air Force Secretary Troy Meink delivered a parallel message Monday at the Air and Space Forces Association's Warfare Symposium at the Gaylord Rockies Resort and Convention Center in Aurora. Meink described reforms to the Air Force's contracting processes, including new portfolio acquisition executives with greater authority to manage risk and resources across programs. The Air Force plans to eventually have 18 such executives and the Space Force nine.

The visits underscore Colorado's growing prominence in the defense space sector. The Department of Defense also released a spending plan Monday indicating it expects to deploy $152 billion from a 2025 reconciliation bill, including $500 million for space launch infrastructure and $7.2 billion for space-based sensor development.

stories.headline: Colorado snowpack still at 63% of normal despite February storms, worst since 1987
stories.summary:
A series of storms that brought as much as 50 inches of snow to parts of Colorado since February 11 has improved conditions at ski resorts but left the state's overall snowpack at just 63 percent of the 30-year normal, the worst reading at this point in the season since 1987. National Weather Service meteorologist Brianna Bealo said late-season storms help but are unlikely to bring snowpack close to median levels.

Ski resorts saw meaningful improvement from the recent precipitation. Purgatory Resort in southwestern Colorado received more than three feet of snow between February 17 and 20 and now has 94 percent of its terrain open. Vail Resorts properties in Eagle County received about 22 inches and Summit County locations picked up roughly 12 inches.

Colorado's mountain snowpack is a critical water source for downstream communities, farms, ranches and hydropower operations once it begins melting in April and May. A below-normal snowpack raises concerns for water managers across the state and in downstream states that depend on Colorado River water.

'Getting these late-season storms is going to help,' Bealo said. 'But there's only so much we can do this late in the season. We probably won't get anywhere close to median.' Resort officials credited heavy investments in snowmaking systems with keeping operations viable during the lean early season.

stories.headline: Solar arrays cover less than 1% of Colorado farmland despite rural opposition, industry study finds
stories.summary:
Ground-mounted solar projects covered 14,326 acres across 39 Colorado counties as of the end of 2024, representing a fraction of 1 percent of the state's 30.2 million acres of farmland, according to a study released by the Colorado Solar and Storage Association. The finding pushes back against widespread concerns in rural communities that renewable energy development is consuming significant agricultural land.

Utility-scale solar projects of 1 megawatt or more accounted for 12,601 of those acres. An additional 6,700 acres of solar projects were added in 2025. The study found that between 1972 and 2000, 2 million acres were converted to rural subdivisions in Colorado, and that suburban sprawl rather than solar development is the dominant driver of farmland loss.

Despite the relatively small footprint, solar development has triggered significant local resistance in several Colorado counties. Projects in Delta County, San Miguel County and La Plata County were blocked, reworked or abandoned in the face of community opposition. A dozen counties imposed solar moratoriums while developing land-use regulations, and at least three remain in effect.

KC Becker, CEO of the Colorado Solar and Storage Association, said the study was motivated by persistent claims that renewable energy was causing major harm in rural areas. 'Farmland isn't being lost to renewable energy, it's being lost to suburban sprawl,' she said. The legislature passed a bill in 2024 directing state agencies to evaluate local permitting processes for solar projects.

stories.headline: Elitch Gardens opens applications for 1,500 seasonal positions, to hold hiring fairs this weekend
stories.summary:
Elitch Gardens announced Tuesday it has opened applications for approximately 1,500 seasonal positions ahead of its planned opening in April, with two hiring fairs scheduled for this weekend at the park's Front Entry Pavilion at 2000 Elitch Circle in Denver.

The fairs will run from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. Saturday and noon to 5 p.m. Sunday. Applicants must be at least 16 years old and can interview on-site for positions spanning rides, aquatics, retail, food service, security and maintenance departments. Starting pay is $19.29 per hour, with benefits including free park admission on off days and complimentary tickets for friends and family.

The park is scheduled to open April 11 for Season Pass Appreciation Weekend, with general public admission beginning April 18. General Manager Rick Howarth said the jobs offer employees practical skills development and professional growth opportunities alongside competitive wages.

Elitch Gardens, which has operated in Denver for more than a century, is one of the city's largest seasonal employers. Applications are available at ElitchGardens.com/Jobs.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
